channel.title: calebssportsblog
channel.description: This blog will be about projects I worked on in 350

item.title: Dylan Carey has been the most impactful player in the big ten
item.dc:creator: Caleb Juedes
item.description:
If you have been keeping up with the Nebraska Cornhusker baseball team you would know that this season has been one to remember. The Cornhuskers currently hold a 33-11 record and a 17-4 record in conference play. The Huskers have had plenty of bright spots on the young season but the biggest bright spot would be the play Nebraska is getting from their All-American shortstop, Dylan Carey. Carey has been the do-it-all man for Nebraska. Carey is a lifetime Husker and has been a part of many memorable teams, but Carey is trying to do something he has not accomplished yet in his career. That goal, of course, would be to lead his team to a College World Series title.

First, lets look at how Dylan Carey stacks among other Husker hitters. These stats will be for the whole season, meaning non-conference and conference games are grouped together.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)
neb <- read_csv("nebraska_baseball_2026.csv")

ne_baseball <- neb|>
filter(G > 5)|>
filter(PA >25)

dylan_carey <- ne_baseball |>
  filter(Name== "Dylan Carey")

bar1<- ggplot()+
  geom_bar(data = ne_baseball,aes(x=reorder(Name,BA), weight=BA))+
  geom_bar(data = dylan_carey,aes(x=reorder(Name,BA), weight=BA), fill="red")+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(
    title = "Dylan Carey Leads the Charge",
    subtitle = "Dylan Carey has been someone the nationally-ranked Huskers have relied on heavily this season",
    caption = "",
    x = "Player",
    y = "Batting Average"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )


bar2<- ggplot()+
  geom_bar(data = ne_baseball,aes(x=reorder(Name,HR), weight=HR))+
  geom_bar(data = dylan_carey,aes(x=reorder(Name,HR), weight=HR), fill="red")+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(
    title = "",
    subtitle = "",
    caption = "",
    x = "Player",
    y = "Homeruns"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

bar3 <- ggplot()+
  geom_bar(data = ne_baseball,aes(x=reorder(Name,RBI), weight=RBI))+
  geom_bar(data = dylan_carey,aes(x=reorder(Name,RBI), weight=RBI), fill="red")+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(
    title = "",
    subtitle = "",
    caption = "",
    x = "Player",
    y = "Runs Batted In"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

bar4 <- ggplot()+
  geom_bar(data = ne_baseball,aes(x=reorder(Name,OPS), weight=OPS))+
  geom_bar(data = dylan_carey,aes(x=reorder(Name,OPS), weight=OPS), fill="red")+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(
    title = "",
    subtitle = "",
    caption = "Caleb Juedes Source: Sports Reference",
    x = "Player",
    y = "On Base Plus Slugging"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 5),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot" )

plot_grid (bar1, bar2, bar3, bar4)

Looking at some of those charts, it is pretty clear that Carey is leading the huskers in many of the offensive categories. Above are four different charts. Batting average, on base plus slugging, runs batted in, and batting average are the stats we will look at. Carey leads Nebraska in home runs, RBIs, and on base plus slugging. He is also second in batting average. The red line highlighted is Carey. The x-axis is always the same, which is the players, but the y-axis differs among the charts. Team stats are very good stats to compare among the team, but as we all know team stats do not tell the whole story sometimes. Players can just be having a good year while there team is struggling, so it may under some circumstances make Carey look better.

In the first charts we looked at how Dylan Carey did among other Cornhusker hitters. We will now look at some data that gets Big Ten foes involved.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

baseball <- read_csv("bigtenproject.csv")

big10 <- baseball|>
  filter(PA >100) |>
  filter(RBI>35)|>
  filter(BA>.300)|>
  filter(HR >5)|>
  select(Name,HR,RBI,BA,OPS)|>
  mutate_at(vars(-Name), as.numeric) |>
  select(Name,HR,RBI,BA,OPS)|>
  arrange(desc(RBI))
 big10 |>
  gt()|>
  cols_label(
    HR= "Home Runs",
    RBI= "Runs Batted In",
    BA= "Batting Average",
    OPS= "On Base Plus Slugging"
     ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Dylan Carey is Leading the Loaded Big Ten in RBIs",
    subtitle= "Even when the scounting reports are all over Carey, he always answers the bell"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("**By:** Caleb Juedes  |  **Source:** Sports Reference: Basketball data set for R Package for Men's Basketball Data")
    ) |>
  tab_style(
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
    style = list(
      cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
      cell_text(weight = "bold", size = 12)
    )
  )|>
  tab_style(
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
    style = list(
      cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
      cell_text(weight = "bold", size = 12)
    )
  ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  opt_table_lines("none") |>
fmt_number(
    columns = c(RBI),
    decimals = 0
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "red"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
    ),
    locations = cells_body(columns = RBI)
  )
Dylan Carey is Leading the Loaded Big Ten in RBIs
Even when the scounting reports are all over Carey, he always answers the bell
Name Home Runs Runs Batted In Batting Average On Base Plus Slugging
Dylan Carey 12 51 0.390 1.155
Will Gasparino 15 49 0.352 1.159
Mulivai Levu* 10 46 0.348 1.032
Michael Anderson 16 44 0.375 1.300
Drew Smith 13 44 0.373 1.179
Roch Cholowsky 13 43 0.324 1.108
Sam Flores* 9 43 0.377 1.095
Dane Harvey* 12 41 0.308 1.065
Jake Hanley* 11 39 0.338 1.046
Roman Martin 6 39 0.331 1.005
AJ Putty 7 39 0.333 0.969
Jett Buck 7 37 0.327 0.938
Weber Neels 9 36 0.338 1.028
By: Caleb Juedes | Source: Sports Reference: Basketball data set for R Package for Men’s Basketball Data

Looking at this chart, we can see that Dylan Carey is not only leading the huskers, he is also leading all of the Big Ten conference in RBIs. Runs batted in are not always going to result in helping your team win, but they certainly can help the cause. When runners are getting on base, Carey is doing his job and bringing in the Huskers that are in scoring position. When Carey steps foot to the plate, many husker fans do not worry as they have seen the Husker senior come through time and time again.

Dylan Carey usually hits cleanup for Nebraska, presenting him the opportunity for many RBI chances. In red I have highlighted the leaders in RBIs. RBIs are a good tool to measure offensive success, but I believe there is still one better way to measure offensive production. In the next chart, we will look at batting average, on base plus slugging, and how Dylan Carey ranks in the Big Ten Conference.

Code 
library(tidyverse)

ranking <- read_csv("bigtenproject.csv")

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=ranking, aes(x=BA, y=OPS), color="gray") +
  geom_point(
    data = subset(ranking, Name == "Dylan Carey"),
    aes(x=BA, y=OPS), color="red", size=3
  )+
  geom_text(
    data = subset(ranking, Name == "Dylan Carey"),
    aes(x=BA, y=OPS, label = "Dylan Carey"),
    vjust = -1,
    size = 3
  )+
  geom_smooth(data=ranking, aes(x=BA, y=OPS), method="lm")+ 
theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    y = "On Base Plus Slugging",
    x = "Batting Average",
    title = "Dylan Carey Hitting for Average and Power",
    subtitle = "Many players can hit for either power or contact, great players can do both",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | By Caleb Juedes"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

Looking at this chart, we can tell that Dylan Carey is not only hitting the baseball well this season, he is hitting it for power and extra base hits. Players can have a high batting average and might just be a contact hitter, but this chart shows Carey is in the top right, meaning he is hitting for average and for power. This makes it very difficult for pitchers to get the right-handed hitter out. Driving the ball to all parts of the field is something that Carey does well. Looking at the stats, we can see whatever the Huskers need Carey to do he usually comes up big for them.

Leading any conference in any offensive category is very hard to do, but to be leading a conference in multiple aspects is just something that is not very common, especially in a conference as tough as the Big Ten. Carey is no stranger to these tall tasks and will openly attack whatever challenge is thrown at this Huskers squad.

If Nebraska wants to continue their success and keep this season going, the Huskers are going to need Carey and their other seniors to lead the way. Baseball is a very unforgiving game and tomorrow is a new day meaning it could be anyone’s year.

item.title: Pryce Sandfort was the best shooter in the Big ten
item.dc:creator: Caleb Juedes
item.description:
This year for Nebraska men basketball fans was a year to remember. Coming into the year fans were optimistic as always but this year was different. The huskers were projected towards the bottom half of the big ten coming into the year.

The Nebraska Cornhuskers were looking to build off the momentum the team had captured at the end of the previous season. Nebraska would be led by incoming transfers of likes such as Jamarques Lawerence returning back home and newcomer Pryce Sandfort.

Anyone watching the Nebraska mens seasson saw a completed different team. Pryce Sandfort lead the Cornhuskers to a historic year and made the schools first ever sweet 16 appearance. Its not a crazy statement to say that Pryce Sandfort was one of the most valuable players in college basketball this season.

We are going to look at some data and have some visuals to back up the argument.The data we will be looking at will all have came from the same place. Sports Reference college basketball is the site I used. Sports Reference had multiple fields to choose from. Throughout this blog post I will be looking at Sandfort against this college teammates and then look at a bigger picture such as how he faired compared to the rest of the Big Ten.

The first visualization we will look at is going to be some bar charts comparing to Pryce Sandfort stats to the rest of the Nebraska Cornhuskers. You can look at any field you are wanting to look at but for this post since I am telling people he is the best shooter in college basketball, I will need to look at shooting metrics to see these results. Looking at the charts you can tell that Pryce Sandfort lead the team in almost all of the offensive categories. According to the charts we can see that Pryce has a sizable lead on the next closest husker.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.3.3
Warning: package 'lubridate' was built under R version 4.3.3
Code 
nebraskaball<- read_csv("Nebraskaball.csv")|>
filter( MP > 5)|>
filter(G > 10)



Pryce_sandfort <- nebraskaball |>
  filter(
    Player== "Pryce Sandfort"
  )

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=nebraskaball, aes(x=reorder(Player, PTS), weight=PTS)) + 
  geom_bar(data=Pryce_sandfort, aes(x=reorder(Player, PTS), weight=PTS), fill="red")+
  coord_flip()+
labs(
    title = "Pryce Sandfort lead the charge for the Huskers on the run to the sweet 16",
    subtitle = "Pryce Sandfort lead Nebraska in just about every offensive catergory",
    caption = "Caleb Juedes Source: Sports Reference",
    x = "Player",
    y = "Pts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )
Code 
 ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=nebraskaball, aes(x=reorder(Player, FT), weight=FT)) + 
  geom_bar(data=Pryce_sandfort, aes(x=reorder(Player, FT), weight=FT), fill="red")+
  coord_flip()+ 
  labs(
    title = "Pryce Sandfort lead the charge for the Huskers on the run to the sweet 16",
    subtitle = "Pryce Sandfort and how his free throws matchup against the other huskers",
    caption = "Caleb Juedes Source: Sports Reference",
    x = "Player",
    y = "Pts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )
Code 
 ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=nebraskaball, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`)) + 
  geom_bar(data=Pryce_sandfort, aes(x=reorder(Player, `3P`), weight=`3P`), fill="red")+
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Pryce Sandfort lead the charge for the Huskers on the run to the sweet 16",
    subtitle = "Pryce Sandfort and how his three pointers matchup against the other huskers",
    caption = "Caleb Juedes Source: Sports Reference",
    x = "Player",
    y = "Pts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )
Code 
ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(data=nebraskaball, aes(x=reorder(Player, `2P`), weight=`2P`)) + 
  geom_bar(data=Pryce_sandfort, aes(x=reorder(Player, `2P`), weight=`2P`), fill="red")+
  coord_flip()+
  labs(
    title = "Pryce Sandfort lead the charge for the Huskers on the run to the sweet 16",
    subtitle = "Pryce Sandfort and how his two pointers matchup against the other huskers",
    caption = "Caleb Juedes Source: Sports Reference",
    x = "Player",
    y = "Pts"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
  )

Comparing Pryce against other Huskers is great if we are just saying that Pryce was the best shooter on Nebraska team but I believe this argument is much deeper. The Big Ten Conference is one of the hardest conferences to get wins in night in and night out and the Huskers were able to capture that energy most nights. To beat good teams in the Big Ten you most play good also. Lets take a look at a few visualization that show how Pryce Sandfort stacks amongst other Big Ten foes.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
Warning: package 'gt' was built under R version 4.3.3
Code 
logs <- read_csv("Big_ten_2026.csv")
Rows: 240 Columns: 31
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr  (5): Player, Team, Pos, Awards, Player-additional
dbl (26): Rk, G, GS, MP, FG, FGA, FG%, 3P, 3PA, 3P%, 2P, 2PA, 2P%, eFG%, FT,...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
Code 
head(logs)
# A tibble: 6 × 31
     Rk Player Team  Pos       G    GS    MP    FG   FGA `FG%`  `3P` `3PA` `3P%`
  <dbl> <chr>  <chr> <chr> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1     1 Nick … Nort… F        33    33  1175   268   526 0.51     45   108 0.417
2     2 Benne… Iowa  G        37    37  1396   251   526 0.477    92   257 0.358
3     3 Nick … Wisc… G        35    35  1117   252   525 0.48     61   167 0.365
4     4 Bruce… Ohio… G        34    34  1241   237   428 0.554    62   155 0.4  
5     5 Lamar… Indi… G        32    32  1087   227   490 0.463   104   275 0.378
6     6 Keato… Illi… G        37    37  1255   202   454 0.445    87   219 0.397
# ℹ 18 more variables: `2P` <dbl>, `2PA` <dbl>, `2P%` <dbl>, `eFG%` <dbl>,
#   FT <dbl>, FTA <dbl>, `FT%` <dbl>, ORB <dbl>, DRB <dbl>, TRB <dbl>,
#   AST <dbl>, STL <dbl>, BLK <dbl>, TOV <dbl>, PF <dbl>, PTS <dbl>,
#   Awards <chr>, `Player-additional` <chr>
Code 
big10<- logs|>
  filter(`3PA` > 100)|>
  select(Player,`2P%`,`2PA`,`3P%`,`3PA`) |>
  mutate_at(vars(-Player), as.numeric) |>
  select(Player,`2PA`,`2P%`,`3PA`,`3P%`)|>
  arrange(desc(`3P%`))
Code 
big10 |> gt()
Player 2PA 2P% 3PA 3P%
Tyler Bilodeau 215 0.553 138 0.464
Fletcher Loyer 110 0.491 259 0.432
Skyy Clark 104 0.519 124 0.427
Nick Martinelli 418 0.533 108 0.417
Pryce Sandfort 143 0.615 310 0.416
Cade Tyson 210 0.571 172 0.413
John Mobley Jr. 123 0.472 236 0.411
Elijah Saunders 95 0.484 141 0.411
Jake Davis 14 0.643 133 0.406
Cooper Koch 58 0.534 164 0.402
Braeden Carrington 34 0.441 172 0.401
Bruce Thornton 273 0.641 155 0.400
Keaton Wagler 235 0.489 219 0.397
Trent Perry 178 0.466 120 0.392
Bobby Durkin 53 0.585 204 0.392
Trey McKenney 99 0.586 174 0.391
Eli Rice 51 0.373 105 0.390
John Blackwell 214 0.477 247 0.389
Jaxon Kohler 179 0.592 149 0.389
Chad Baker-Mazara 178 0.500 160 0.381
Kur Teng 93 0.376 147 0.381
Nick Dorn 20 0.600 158 0.380
Lamar Wilkerson 215 0.572 275 0.378
Elliot Cadeau 158 0.449 173 0.376
David Mirkovic 212 0.561 152 0.375
Nimari Burnett 87 0.598 160 0.375
C.J. Cox 92 0.609 166 0.373
Yaxel Lendeborg 208 0.639 180 0.372
Nick Boyd 358 0.534 167 0.365
Austin Rapp 69 0.551 160 0.363
Braden Smith 271 0.491 177 0.362
Ben Humrichous 25 0.520 158 0.361
Bennett Stirtz 269 0.591 257 0.358
TK Simpkins 158 0.487 140 0.357
Jamarques Lawrence 116 0.509 155 0.355
Omer Mayer 84 0.488 110 0.355
Braden Frager 113 0.681 162 0.352
Tariq Francis 284 0.454 148 0.351
Quimari Peterson 62 0.516 203 0.350
Dominick Stewart 60 0.500 112 0.348
Andre Mills 171 0.468 159 0.346
Harun Zrno 40 0.425 154 0.338
Tucker DeVries 117 0.530 246 0.333
Alvaro Folgueiras 102 0.676 108 0.333
David Coit 143 0.545 219 0.324
Jaylen Crocker-Johnson 140 0.500 130 0.323
Jeremy Fears Jr. 255 0.478 109 0.321
Nathan Bittle 199 0.548 113 0.319
Freddie Dilione V 236 0.559 132 0.318
Andrew Rohde 50 0.480 132 0.318
Jayden Reid 191 0.429 101 0.317
Jackson Shelstad 64 0.516 105 0.314
Tomislav Ivisic 111 0.730 160 0.313
Wesley Yates III 140 0.443 170 0.312
Rienk Mast 221 0.538 161 0.311
Kylan Boswell 161 0.540 114 0.307
Kwame Evans Jr. 169 0.544 102 0.304
Isaac Asuma 126 0.540 191 0.304
Wei Lin 75 0.413 105 0.295
Darius Adams 181 0.431 133 0.248
Code 
big10 |>
  gt()|>
  cols_label(
    `2PA`= "2 point shot attempts",
    `2P%`= "2 point shooting percentage",
    `3PA`= "3 point shot attempts",
    `3P%`= "3 point shooting percentage"
  )
Player 2 point shot attempts 2 point shooting percentage 3 point shot attempts 3 point shooting percentage
Tyler Bilodeau 215 0.553 138 0.464
Fletcher Loyer 110 0.491 259 0.432
Skyy Clark 104 0.519 124 0.427
Nick Martinelli 418 0.533 108 0.417
Pryce Sandfort 143 0.615 310 0.416
Cade Tyson 210 0.571 172 0.413
John Mobley Jr. 123 0.472 236 0.411
Elijah Saunders 95 0.484 141 0.411
Jake Davis 14 0.643 133 0.406
Cooper Koch 58 0.534 164 0.402
Braeden Carrington 34 0.441 172 0.401
Bruce Thornton 273 0.641 155 0.400

Omer Mayer 84 0.488 110 0.355

Tariq Francis 284 0.454 148 0.351
Quimari Peterson 62 0.516 203 0.350
Dominick Stewart 60 0.500 112 0.348

Alvaro Folgueiras 102 0.676 108 0.333

Jeremy Fears Jr. 255 0.478 109 0.321
Nathan Bittle 199 0.548 113 0.319
Freddie Dilione V 236 0.559 132 0.318
Andrew Rohde 50 0.480 132 0.318
Jayden Reid 191 0.429 101 0.317
Jackson Shelstad 64 0.516 105 0.314
Tomislav Ivisic 111 0.730 160 0.313
Wesley Yates III 140 0.443 170 0.312
Rienk Mast 221 0.538 161 0.311
Kylan Boswell 161 0.540 114 0.307
Kwame Evans Jr. 169 0.544 102 0.304
Isaac Asuma 126 0.540 191 0.304
Wei Lin 75 0.413 105 0.295
Darius Adams 181 0.431 133 0.248
Code 
big10 |>
  gt()|>
  cols_label(
    `2PA`= "2 point shot attempts",
    `2P%`= "2 point shooting percentage",
    `3PA`= "3 point shot attempts",
    `3P%`= "3 point shooting percentage"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Pryce Sandfort is shooting the three at a very high rate this season",
    subtitle= "Pryce Sandfort is in the top shooters in the big ten percentage based off this three point percantage and attempts this year"
  )
Pryce Sandfort is shooting the three at a very high rate this season
Pryce Sandfort is in the top shooters in the big ten percentage based off this three point percantage and attempts this year
Player 2 point shot attempts 2 point shooting percentage 3 point shot attempts 3 point shooting percentage
Tyler Bilodeau 215 0.553 138 0.464
Fletcher Loyer 110 0.491 259 0.432
Skyy Clark 104 0.519 124 0.427
Nick Martinelli 418 0.533 108 0.417
Pryce Sandfort 143 0.615 310 0.416
Cade Tyson 210 0.571 172 0.413
John Mobley Jr. 123 0.472 236 0.411
Elijah Saunders 95 0.484 141 0.411
Jake Davis 14 0.643 133 0.406
Cooper Koch 58 0.534 164 0.402
Braeden Carrington 34 0.441 172 0.401
Bruce Thornton 273 0.641 155 0.400
Keaton Wagler 235 0.489 219 0.397

Code 
big10 |>
  gt()|>
  cols_label(
    `2PA`= "2 point shot attempts",
    `2P%`= "2 point shooting percentage",
    `3PA`= "3 point shot attempts",
    `3P%`= "3 point shooting percentage"
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = "Pryce Sandfort is shooting the three at a very high rate this season",
    subtitle= "Pryce Sandfort is in the top shooters in the big ten percentage based off this three point percantage and attempts this year." 
  )|>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |> tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle"))
